content.problemStatement:
You are given a tree with NNN nodes rooted at node 111.

The ithi^{th}ith node of the tree has a value AiA_iAi​ assigned to it.

For the ithi^{th}ith node (1≤i≤N)(1\le i \le N)(1≤i≤N), output the **minimum** value of bitwise XOR of the **values** of any two **distinct** nodes in the subtree of node iii.

Formally, for each node iii, find the value of min(Ax⊕Ay)min(A_x \oplus A_y)min(Ax​⊕Ay​), where x≠yx \ne yx=y and x,y∈x, y \in x,y∈ subtree of node iii.

Note that since leaf nodes have only 111 node in their subtree, output −1-1−1 for all the leaf nodes.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case begins with a single line of input, an integer NNN - the number of nodes in the tree.
The following (N−1)(N-1)(N−1) lines each contain two integers, UUU and VVV, denoting an undirected edge between nodes UUU and VVV.
The last line of each test case contains NNN integers. The ithi^{th}ith integer is the value assigned to ithi^{th}ith node.

It is assured that the edges given in the input form a tree.



Output Format
For each test case, output NNN integers where the ithi^{th}ith integer is the **minimum** value of bitwise XOR of the **values** of any two **distinct** nodes in the subtree of node iii.

If node iii is a leaf node, output −1-1−1 instead.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N – the number of nodes in the tree. The next N‑1 lines each contain two integers U and V denoting an undirected edge. The last line contains N integers A1 … AN, where Ai is the value of node i. The tree is rooted at node 1.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‑separated integers. The i‑th integer is the minimum XOR of values of any two distinct nodes inside the subtree of node i. If node i is a leaf, output -1 for that position.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There is only one node in the tree which is a leaf node. Hence, the output is −1-1−1.
Test case 222: The tree formed by the input of second test case is shown below:


Nodes 222 and Node 333 are leaf nodes. Hence, the answer for them is −1-1−1.
For the subtree formed by node 111, the answer is min((4⊕5),(5⊕6),(4⊕6))min((4\oplus5),(5\oplus6), (4\oplus6))min((4⊕5),(5⊕6),(4⊕6)) which is min(1,3,2)=1min(1, 3, 2) = 1min(1,3,2)=1.

solution.approach: Perform a DFS from the root. For each node we keep a binary trie (bits 0‑49 because Ai ≤ 10^15) that stores all values in its subtree and a vector with those values. The answer for a leaf is -1. For an internal node we start with a trie containing only its own value. Then for each child we merge the child's data into the current node using small‑to‑large: iterate over the smaller vector, for each value query the minimum XOR against the larger trie (this gives a candidate for the answer), insert the value into the larger trie, and finally append the value to the larger vector. After processing all children the stored answer is the minimum XOR found; if the subtree size is 1 we keep -1. The total work is O(N·log C) where C = 2^50, because each value is inserted and queried O(log C) times and each value moves from a smaller to a larger container at most O(log N) times, which is bounded by the same logarithmic factor. Memory usage is O(N·log C) for the trie nodes.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N · log C) per test case (C ≤ 2^50, i.e., about 50 bits).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N · log C) for the trie nodes plus O(N) for auxiliary vectors.
